patient.name: Anika Patel
patient.chiefComplaint: Severe allergic reaction with airway swelling after shellfish exposure

history.hpi: 42-year-old female at a seafood restaurant for a birthday dinner. Took a bite of a shared appetizer not realizing it contained shrimp paste. Known severe shellfish allergy with prior ED visits. Within minutes developed tongue swelling, throat tightness, and urticaria. Administered two doses of her own 0.3 mg epinephrine auto-injector 5 minutes apart with minimal improvement. Her husband called 911.
pastMedical: Severe shellfish allergy with anaphylaxis (2 prior ED visits)
history.lastOralIntake: Single bite of shrimp paste dumpling approximately 10 minutes before EMS arrival
history.events: Ate one bite of shared appetizer containing hidden shellfish. Rapid onset of throat tightness, tongue swelling, and hives. Self-administered 2 epi auto-injectors. Symptoms progressed despite treatment.

scene.dispatch: Medic 4, respond code 3 to Fortune Palace Restaurant at 2100 Grand Avenue, for a 42-year-old female with severe allergic reaction, difficulty breathing.
scene.location: Crowded restaurant dining room. Patient seated at a table near the center of the room with a large birthday party. Close-quarters working environment.
scene.safety: Scene safe. Need to clear tables and move patient to a wider space or extricate to the ambulance promptly.
scene.bystanders: Husband and birthday party guests present. Husband is calm and informative, hands over both used auto-injectors and confirms timing and dose.
visualCues: Patient sitting forward, visibly struggling to breathe, gripping the table
Massive tongue swelling protruding from the mouth
Audible high-pitched stridor with each inspiration
Diffuse urticaria on the chest, arms, and face
Two spent EpiPen auto-injectors on the table

phases.name: Arrival — Refractory Anaphylaxis with Closing Airway
phases.description: Crew arrives to a dramatic airway emergency. Massive angioedema with obvious tongue and facial swelling. Stridor barely audible — air movement is critically limited. SpO2 is 74%. Two IM epi doses from the patient's own auto-injectors have not halted progression. Time to definitive airway is measured in minutes.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Massive tongue edema protruding from mouth; audible inspiratory stridor; minimal air movement
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Rapid shallow respirations with obvious tripoding; accessory muscle use; unable to speak sentences

skin.color: dusky cyanosis of lips, diffuse urticaria on trunk and face

clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Anxious, purposeful movements, gripping the table
otherFindings: Massive lip, tongue, and periorbital edema
Diffuse hives on chest, arms, face
Stridor so severe that each breath is visible as neck retraction

expectedActions.action: Allow patient to remain in position of comfort (upright, tripod)
Administer Epinephrine 1 mg/mL (1:1,000) 0.3 mg IM in the lateral thigh immediately — do not wait for IV access
expectedActions.rationale: IM epi is first-line in anaphylaxis; a third dose is appropriate despite the patient's two prior doses, which may have been subtherapeutic or poorly delivered.

expectedActions.action: Apply high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather at 15 LPM while preparing airway equipment
Administer nebulized Epinephrine 1 mg/mL, 5 mL via nebulizer mask for stridor
Establish two large-bore IVs; one for fluid, one for medications
Administer 20 mL/kg isotonic fluid bolus (normal saline, 1360 mL for 68 kg patient) rapidly over 15 minutes
Administer Diphenhydramine 1 mg/kg IV (68 mg for 68 kg patient, max 50 mg — so give 50 mg) IV push
Administer Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV OR Dexamethasone 10 mg IV push
Apply cardiac monitor, SpO2, waveform capnography

phases.name: Go/No-Go Airway Decision
phases.description: A third IM epi has been given. Despite maximal medical therapy, swelling continues to progress. The crew now faces a critical decision: is the airway still passable with an endotracheal tube via video laryngoscopy, or has it progressed to the point where the ETT will not pass and a surgical airway is the primary plan? The crew must commit to a double-setup: simultaneous preparation for both attempts.
phases.triggerCondition: Stridor worsening or SpO2 not improving despite third IM epi

transitions.condition: Crew decides to attempt ETT and visualizes cords with video laryngoscope
Airway cannot be visualized; surgical airway is indicated

clinicalPresentation.airway: Tongue edema worsening, stridor now nearly silent — critical loss of air movement
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Weak thready pulse, BP trending down despite IM epi
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Beginning to lose purposeful movement, head bobbing
otherFindings: Airway is visibly swollen externally with distorted landmarks
Cricothyroid membrane is palpable but surrounded by edema

expectedActions.action: Administer third Epinephrine 0.3 mg IM in the lateral thigh (or 0.1 mg IV push if code situation)
Prepare Epinephrine IV drip at 0.5 mcg/kg/min (34 mcg/min for 68 kg patient) given cardiovascular collapse despite multiple IM doses
Double-setup: assign one provider to prepare RSI drugs and VL equipment while a second provider preps cricothyrotomy kit and marks the cricothyroid membrane
Prepare smaller-than-usual ETT (6.0 mm) anticipating edematous airway
Continue preoxygenation with BVM + PEEP and apneic oxygenation NC 15 LPM
Administer Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg IV push (100 mg for 68 kg patient); ketamine preserves respiratory drive and hemodynamics
Administer Rocuronium 1 mg/kg IV push (68 mg for 68 kg patient); use longer-acting paralytic to avoid re-paralysis if multiple attempts required
Attempt single best-effort video laryngoscopy intubation with 6.0 mm ETT and bougie; limit attempt to single pass
If cords cannot be visualized or tube cannot be passed, proceed immediately to surgical cricothyrotomy without additional attempts

phases.name: Successful Endotracheal Intubation
phases.description: A single video laryngoscopy attempt with a small ETT and bougie succeeds. Tube confirmed. Ventilation improves dramatically. Ongoing anaphylaxis treatment continues.
phases.triggerCondition: ETT successfully placed with confirmed waveform capnography

clinicalPresentation.airway: 6.0 mm ETT in place at 21 cm, cuff inflated, secured
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Ventilated via BVM with symmetric chest rise
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Perfusing with ongoing epi drip and fluids
otherFindings: Facial edema unchanged externally but airway is bypassed

expectedActions.action: Confirm tube placement with waveform capnography, bilateral breath sounds, absent epigastric sounds
Continue Epinephrine IV drip at 0.5 mcg/kg/min (titrate to MAP 65); titrate down as patient stabilizes
Continue isotonic fluid boluses up to 60 mL/kg (4,080 mL total) for ongoing distributive shock
Post-intubation sedation: Ketamine 0.5-1 mg/kg IV (34-68 mg) for maintenance; continued analgesia with fentanyl
Ventilate at 12 breaths/min, 6-8 mL/kg IBW, EtCO2 target 35-45
Emergent transport to closest appropriate ED with early notification

phases.name: Branch: Surgical Cricothyrotomy
phases.description: Attempt at video laryngoscopy reveals that the airway is completely obliterated by edema. The provider commits immediately to surgical cricothyrotomy through the already-marked cricothyroid membrane.
phases.triggerCondition: Laryngoscopy reveals no identifiable cords or tube cannot be passed

clinicalPresentation.airway: 6.0 mm cuffed tube placed through cricothyroid membrane, secured
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Ventilated via BVM through surgical airway
otherFindings: Small amount of bleeding at surgical site controlled with direct pressure

expectedActions.action: Confirm cricothyrotomy placement with waveform capnography, symmetric chest rise, bilateral breath sounds
Secure surgical airway carefully — it is more prone to dislodgement than ETT
Continue epinephrine drip and fluid resuscitation
Notify receiving ED of surgical airway — ENT/trauma team should meet the ambulance

criticalActions: Administer Epinephrine 0.3 mg IM (1 mg/mL) immediately on recognition despite two prior patient-administered doses
Nebulized Epinephrine 5 mL of 1 mg/mL for stridor
Establish IV access and begin 20 mL/kg NS bolus
High-flow oxygen preparation for imminent airway management
Recognize refractory anaphylaxis and initiate Epinephrine IV drip 0.5 mcg/kg/min
Double-setup for definitive airway: prepare ETT (smaller size) AND cricothyrotomy kit simultaneously
Perform RSI with Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg + Rocuronium 1 mg/kg
If ETT not possible, commit to surgical cricothyrotomy
Confirm advanced airway with waveform capnography
expectedActions: Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV or Dexamethasone 10 mg IV
Mark cricothyroid membrane prior to RSI attempt
Early ED notification for ENT/trauma resources
bonusActions: Articulate double-setup rationale in front-line team
Choose smaller ETT (6.0 mm) anticipating edema
Use bougie as adjunct for difficult airway

learningObjectives: Apply the MATC Anaphylaxis protocol including IM epi, nebulized epi, fluids, and IV epi drip progression
Manage a rapidly progressing difficult airway with a double-setup approach
Decide when to commit to surgical airway
Understand indications for IV epinephrine infusion in anaphylaxis
discussionQuestions: Why might the patient's own auto-injector doses have been subtherapeutic?
When do you transition from IM to IV epinephrine in anaphylaxis?
What is a 'double-setup' and why is it appropriate here?
How do you decide between a smaller ETT and going straight to cricothyrotomy?
What features of the airway examination would push you toward surgical airway over ETT attempt?
commonPitfalls: Withholding additional epinephrine because the patient has already received doses
Delaying RSI preparation while waiting to see if medical therapy works
Multiple unsuccessful intubation attempts instead of committing to surgical airway
Using a standard-sized ETT in an edematous airway
Failing to resuscitate fluid status before RSI induction causes post-induction hypotension
keyTakeaways: Epinephrine is the primary treatment — dose it early, repeat it, escalate to drip
Airway progression in anaphylaxis is measured in minutes; plan for surgical airway from the start
A double-setup is good medicine, not an admission of failure
One best-effort attempt at ETT; if it fails, surgical airway is the answer
